channel.title: (ian)Yuang Chen on (ian)Yuang Chen
channel.description: Recent content in (ian)Yuang Chen on (ian)Yuang Chen

item.title: About
item.description:
Hello! I’m Yuang Chen (陈宇昂), a junior student in Peking University, College of Urban and Environmental Sciences(CUES), majoring in Environmental Science. My current interests in research are quite varied and cross-disciplinary, ranging from regional pollutants transmission (which includes emission-inventory compilation, air modeling and scenario analysis) to Policy analysis of emission from certain industries like cement, fertilizer and municipal waste. Read more about my research in this page.

 

Feel free to contact me via: cya.pku@gmail.com or cya@pku.edu.cn

 

#Personal Life

I was born on 25 July 2000, in Jiangsu Province, China. My hometown was where Yangtze River meets Dong Sea, the longest river and the most important Sea in China, respectively. My childhood happy days of swimming in clean water were gone in 2006 due to substantial factories and tons of pollutions pouring into rivers. And then there was black smoke and people catching diseases like cancer. I’m not sure but I always think my childhood memories of my hometown has something to do with my choosing to read Environmental Science.

Currently I’m a junior student in Peking University. I totally understand that I’m not a genius (geniuses are studying Maths and Physics). And people who study Geography and Environmental Science (while I study them both) really need some “dullness” (I’m not saying my classmates are dull), because it takes so much time to result in a work that may not mean a lot. But with so much work added up, every one can make a contribution, and that’s my understanding of my major and also my way of life.

#Students Union

For the past three years, I’ve devoted large amount of time to services, especially in Students Union. I’m currently the president of students union in CUES, leading a 50-people team. We have host dance party that contains 1000+ people and hold Cultural Festivals that attract official media’s attention.

My understanding of service work is that maybe it doesn’t do much help to my research, if any, but I can find happiness in helping people. When we gather everybody around in Mid-autumn Festival to make Moon cakes, I can see those smiling faces even though this is a simple activity. This has something to do with what I want to study–I can gain happiness when I find my research results can really become policy suggestions that may influence thousands of people. That’s why I love them both, the research and the service work.

#Traveling&Hiking!

As a student of Environmental Geography, traveling&hiking all around the world can be the top 1 reason why I love this major!

The mountains I’ve climbed and places I’ve been:

Beijing: Mount. West, Mount. Yangtai, Mount. Xiang, Miyun Reservoir;

The five mountains: Lu, Heng(both), Tai, Hua.

Tibetan Plateau: Mount Himalaya

And we will visit deserts in central China for field sampling next summer!

item.title: Research
item.description:
I am a junior student at the Resource and Environmental Management Research Center affiliating to College of Urban and Environmental Sciences, Peking University.

 

#Research Focus

  • Regional transformation and transportation of pollutants like ammonia and mercury. This includes emission inventory compilation and transportation model development.
  • Analysis of emission trends associated with energy consumption and related policies, which requires methods like LMDI, I-O analysis, LCA and MFA analysis.
  • Every possible chance for field sampling.
  • Always fighting for new possibilities!

 

#Current Projects

(All results and graphs can be used only after consent, my email is in this page)

 

  • Ammonia Emission Inventory Compilation

    (This work is currently ready for submission to journal ES&T)

    While agriculture and livestock are usually considered the biggest contributors to ammonia emissions, there are satellite images and other agiculture-side model work that say something different. The ammonia emitted from industrial and waste disposal sectors in China is non-negligible, especially in dense-populated urban cities and provinces with heavy industries.

    Also, current ammonia emission inventories mostly use proxy data while point-source data is lacking. Our work provides a detailed high resolution industrial and waste disposal ammonia emission inventory with data from various economic and geographic databases and published lists from Chinese government.

 

  • LMDI Analysis of emission trends of ammonia

    This is a follow-up work of the first project. With detailed ammonia emissions from industrial and waste disposal sectors, we now have the chance to study how does this 3-fold increase come into place. With LMDI analysis together with some regression techniques obtained from Econometrics, we try to explain this increase from a political stand.

 

  • Glacial Mercury Release in Tibetan Plateau

    Final draft (Chinese version) of this work has won a third prize in Challenging cup. (The most renowned scientific competition among college students in China)

    Often, we can hear saying like “glaciers are melting and results in those pollutants inside released”. But no one has ever answer how much of these released pollutants will be. We lay eyes on the Tibetan Plateau, the “Asian Water Tower”, and mercury, the most important heavy metal pollutants that also can travel long distance.

    By estimating the amount of glaciers melting and massive field sampling, we develop a method of calculating glacial mercury release from Tibetan Plateau, which can also be used for other estimation of heavy metal release from high mountain glaciers.
